intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

body.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

draft.title: recipe app for people who hate cooking
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
recipe app for people who hate cooking

Building your first draft…

data.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

facade.hypothesis: Users hate cooking because ingredient lists feel overwhelming—if ingredients appear one-by-one as you scroll instead of all at once, it reduces cognitive load and makes the task feel more manageable.
facade.axisTargeted: Motion and behavior—animated reveal vs instant display
facade.label: Ingredients fade in
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers progressive disclosure and gentle pacing; likely responds well to animated transitions that break tasks into digestible moments.
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers seeing all information upfront; favors efficiency and control over gradual reveal; may find animations distracting or patronizing.

facade.hypothesis: People who hate cooking respond better to ultra-minimal information design—showing only what they need to buy, stripped of narrative flavor text and step descriptions.
facade.label: Just the ingredients list
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers recipes presented as pure data (ingredient list, prep time, one-line method). Lean toward scannable tables and stripped-down card layouts.
facade.rejectImplies: User wants some connective tissue between ingredients and action. Probe next: do they want story, or structured guidance (steps, timing, technique hints)?

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond better to colorful, cheerful interfaces that make the process feel fun rather than laborious
facade.axisTargeted: Color temperature and mood (light vs dark, serious vs joyful)
facade.label: Bright and playful
facade.acceptImplies: Move toward vibrant palettes, upbeat illustrations, warm tones, and whimsical visual language
facade.rejectImplies: Shift toward muted, minimal, or serious aesthetics; consider monochrome or cool-toned design as calming alternative

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond better to interfaces that celebrate speed and ease with bright, affirming feedback—small dopamine hits that make the process feel like progress, not drudgery.
facade.label: Cheerful quick wins
facade.acceptImplies: The app should use celebratory language, progress indicators, and warm bright colors to make cooking feel like a series of micro-victories rather than a chore.
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer a more neutral, no-nonsense tone that gets out of the way—minimalist, understated, even slightly cool in mood to match the 'I'm doing this reluctantly' headspace.

evidence.hypothesis: Users hate cooking because ingredient lists feel overwhelming—if ingredients appear one-by-one as you scroll instead of all at once, it reduces cognitive load and makes the task feel more manageable.
evidence.implication: User prefers progressive disclosure and gentle pacing; likely responds well to animated transitions that break tasks into digestible moments.

facade.hypothesis: For cooking haters, large touch-friendly cards showing one recipe at a time reduce cognitive load and feel less overwhelming than dense lists or small text
facade.axisTargeted: card vs list; spacious vs dense
facade.label: Giant recipe cards
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers generous whitespace and one-thing-at-a-time focus—explore full-screen cards, large buttons, minimal options per view
facade.rejectImplies: User wants to scan many recipes quickly—explore list view, grid thumbnails, or sidebar with recipe preview, density over breathing room

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond better to a voice that's wry and self-aware about food struggles rather than cheerful recipe enthusiasm
facade.label: Sarcastic tone
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into humor about cooking friction: 'Burnt it? Us too.' 'This takes 3 minutes (if you ignore the part where you panic)'
facade.rejectImplies: Avoid sarcasm; use warm, encouraging, non-judgmental tone instead: 'You've got this!' 'Cooking simpler than you think'

axes.label: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
Whether user wants minimal decision-making (curated picks) vs. maximum transparency (nutrition, cost, time breakdowns)
Whether user prefers instant gratification (no waiting, animations) vs. satisfying feedback loops (progress bars, completion celebrations)

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants minimal decision-making (curated picks) vs. maximum transparency (nutrition, cost, time breakdowns)
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers instant gratification (no waiting, animations) vs. satisfying feedback loops (progress bars, completion celebrations)
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond better to gentle, warm visual feedback on ingredients as they're selected or added—a glowing highlight instead of sharp confirmation states—because it feels nurturing rather than mechanical, reducing friction anxiety during the task.
facade.axisTargeted: Motion and Behavior — transitions from static/instant feedback to fluid/gentle visual acknowledgment
facade.label: Soft ingredient glow
facade.acceptImplies: User values warm, encouraging feedback loops; prefers animations that feel supportive over utilitarian; likely responds to interfaces that celebrate small actions without harsh confirmations.
facade.rejectImplies: User finds soft feedback ambiguous or slow; prefers clear, instant, snappy confirmation states; likely wants mechanical efficiency over emotional warmth in task completion.

draft.title: QuickBite – Recipe App for People Who Hate Cooking
draft.summary: A recipe app that strips away complexity and delivers dead-simple meals in under 15 minutes with minimal ingredients and zero fuss.
draft.html:
QuickBite

Meals so simple, you'll actually cook them

Today's Picks

Garlic Butter Pasta

8 min3 ingredients

Pasta, butter, garlic. That's it. Toss, eat, move on with your life.

Egg Fried Rice

10 min5 ingredients

Leftover rice + eggs + soy sauce. Tastes like you tried, but you didn't.

Avocado Toast

5 min2 ingredients

Bread, avocado. Smash, season, done. Breakfast solved.

Hungry Now?

Get 10 more zero-effort recipes tailored to what's in your kitchen.
